title: Sonnet--To A Daisy
content:
Slight as thou art, thou art enough to hide,
         Like all created things, secrets from me,
         And stand a barrier to eternity.
    And I, how can I praise thee well and wide?

    From where I dwell--upon the hither side?
         Thou little veil for so great mystery,
         When shall I penetrate all things and thee,
    And then look back?    For this I must abide,

    Till thou shalt grow and fold and be unfurled
         Literally between me and the world.
                Then I shall drink from in beneath a spring,

    And from a poet's side shall read his book.
         O daisy mine, what will it be to look
                From God's side even of such a simple thing?
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alice Christiana Thompson Meynell represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alice Christiana Thompson Meynell's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
